lesson.menu_title: What Were You Doing and What Did You Do? -- The Debrief
lesson.menu_desc: Reconstruct an event in sequence: was / were + verb-ing for the background, past simple for what happened, with 'when' and 'while' -- and the question that opens a scene: 'What were you doing when...?' -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- What Were You Doing and What Did You Do?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Clara | Lesson 12 -- What Were You Doing and What Did You Do?

listenings.text: Maria, it is Bruno. You asked me to reconstruct the round table in Seoul, so here is my version. At two o'clock, we were setting up the room. Our team was testing the microphones, and the Korean delegation was waiting outside. Everything was ready. Then, at two fifteen, the news arrived: the minister was one hour late. While we were changing the schedule, the rector made a decision. He started the session without her. So the first speaker began at two thirty, not at three. I know you were talking on the phone at that moment, so you missed the beginning. The whole thing worked, but the sequence changed. If you need more details, call me.

Yesterday, I gave my first debrief in English. I used to bring an interpreter to every meeting. For twenty years, someone else was telling my story, and I was sitting in silence next to my own words. Not this time. While the director was listening, I reconstructed the whole thing myself: what we were doing at two o'clock, what happened with the minister, and why the sequence changed. My English was not perfect. I stopped two times, and I asked for one word. But every detail was mine, and my version was accurate. At the end, the director said one sentence: Thank you, that was very clear. In that room, I was not a beginner. I was a leader, and I was speaking for myself.

extra_audio.text: At two o'clock, we were setting up the room, and the team was testing the microphones. At two fifteen, the news arrived: the minister was one hour late. While we were changing the schedule, the rector made a decision. The first speaker began at two thirty, not at three. In the end, the whole thing worked. Only the sequence changed.

reading.rtitle: Two Versions of the Same Night
paras: Last November, a Brazilian delegation visited a university in Lisbon. They signed an important partnership. The next morning, the director asked for a debrief. "Tell me exactly what happened," she said.

Paulo spoke first. "We were waiting in the hall, and then the rector signed the document." It was a short version. It was not wrong, but the director was not happy. "Paulo, that is not a story," she said. "Where are the details?"

Then Sofia spoke. She was a witness too, and she reconstructed the whole thing in sequence. "At nine o'clock, we were waiting in the hall, and the rector was talking to the press. While his assistant was reading the last page, she found a mistake in the date. She clarified it with our lawyer, and they printed a new page. At ten, the rector signed." The director smiled. "That is accurate," she said. "Now I understand the whole thing."
reading.source: Institutional & Travel English -- narrative written for Maria Clara's package

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of this story?
choices: Paulo lied to the director about the signing in Lisbon.
A good debrief reconstructs the sequence with the details -- that is what makes a version accurate.
A delegation should never sign a document with the press in the room.

items: The delegation signed a partnership in Lisbon.
t
"They signed an important partnership."
The director was happy with Paulo's first version.
f
"It was a short version. It was not wrong, but the director was not happy."
Sofia was not at the event.
f
"She was a witness too, and she reconstructed the whole thing in sequence."
The assistant found a mistake in the date.
t
"While his assistant was reading the last page, she found a mistake in the date."
At the end, the director understood the whole story.
t
"That is accurate, she said. Now I understand the whole thing."

bank.label: Reconstructing an event: the background, the moment, the question
items: At two o'clock, we were setting up the room.
I was testing the microphones when the news arrived.
While we were changing the schedule, he made a decision.
What were you doing when it happened?
Were they waiting when you arrived?
The whole thing took one hour.

items.situation: A colleague asks you to reconstruct yesterday's meeting. Start with the background: "At nine o'clock, I was..."
tips: At nine o'clock, I was preparing the agenda.
The background is long: was / were + verb-ing.

items.situation: You need one detail from a witness. Ask the question that opens the scene.
tips: What were you doing when it happened?
In the question, were comes BEFORE you.

items.situation: Two things were happening at the same time in the room. Say it with "while".
tips: While I was taking notes, the rector was talking to the press.

items.situation: The background was calm, and then one short thing happened. Say both in one sentence.
tips: I was reading the report when the phone rang.
when + the short event in past simple.

items.situation: Your version of the story is short. The director wants the details. Add two.
tips: At two fifteen, the news arrived: the minister was one hour late.
A detail is a time, a name, or a number.

items.situation: Close your debrief. Say that your version is correct and complete.
tips: My version is accurate -- I was a witness.
The whole thing took one hour.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: What were you doing when you got the news about the China trip?
Reconstruct your last delegation meeting in sequence. Do not stop for fifteen seconds.
I was not in the room yesterday. Give me the whole thing, with three details.

pressure.text: Background first (was / were + verb-ing), then the event (past simple). when = the short thing. while = the long one. Details make the version accurate.
